speaker: interviewer
text: March 14, 1996. Survivor Steve Lesniak, L-E-S-N-I-A-K. And this is tape four. You were talking about working in Schindler's factory, and the feeling that the war was coming to an end.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I had a number of friends. But naturally, our discussion was always survival and food. And we were thinking if, God's will, we have a chance to survive with Schindlers, because the man was doing really utmost. And we were discussing Schindlers as a man, because we all knew, the man, it's a Nazi. And what he's doing, we never came across something like that during the whole situation from the beginning of time when they-- when the Germans entered Poland. Once, I remember like today, when he entered-- this was practically the end of the war, just two nights before-- and he said to us, the war is coming to end. The Russian are practically only a few miles away from us. And it is a possibility I will have to run away and leave you behind. But I will try to stay as long as possible in order to support you as far as I can. The night before, he enter again the factory. And he said, this is the night where I have to leave. And 11 people, of us, with a truck and his car, they joined him. He loaded up everything what he possesses, under our [? already ?] supervision, too. And they were fleeing the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothes was he wearing when he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it. I didn't see it, but some of my friends told me. He put our prisoner's attire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who the prisoners were who went with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were a number of them, but two I-- I knew. They were a brother and a sister. He passed away, too, not a long time ago. I don't recall his name. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were 11 of our boys. Yes, Richard [? Raen ?] and his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they go with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: They felt-- first of all, one was [? called ?] a truck driver. So he was driving the truck. And we paid so much for this man, anybody felt as honor to go with him. And that's 11 people of our went with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was a very touching moment, because when he was leaving, we knew it is a possibility of survival. But still, we are afraid, if he would leave, what is going to happen to the SS guard? Because they were professional killers.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same day-- the same night when they left, we are looking up. And we got words as the guards on the tower disappear. They are not anymore there. But no one of us dared to go out from, you know, the camp, or from the factory, because we're still [? agony ?] over that. People were killing each other, you know-- the Czechoslovakian army against the German private people. It was an ugly scene going on all over at that time. The night of liberation, early in the morning I walked out, because people were already walking outside. They said, the SS disappeared. Everybody's disappeared. We are left alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A few hours later, three Russian soldiers on horseback arrive in our camp, and they said in Russian, you go home. You're free. This strikes me-- till today, I cannot understand when he mentioned those words, you go home. We all knew we had no homes anymore. We are left alone. A few hours later, arrived a underground-- the Czechoslovakian underground and they brought us a lot of food with carriages, wagons, all kinds, in order for to distribute between the prisoners. And he said, you can walk out now to the city. And whenever you will see a white flag, you can enter the house, take whatever you want, and do whatever you want. And I went with a group of boys to one of those houses, where was the white flag. And through all my days, and months, and years when I was dreaming to avenge the death of my parents, my sister and my family and my people, I was living with it. This boost me up, this get me-- got me power to survive. And I entered a house liked that. And right in the corner was standing a young woman, close to 30, with a three, four-year-old girl holding, and shaking. It was a German woman with a German child. I walked out ashamed, because I couldn't do anything wrong to her at that time. And I always couldn't understand my approach-- even that time where I could avenge my people, my family-- I couldn't do anything wrong to anybody else. I was still human. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Till today, I cannot explain myself the feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your feelings-- how do you feel about Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am grateful to the memory of this man. Like I said before, my mother gave me life the first time. Oskar Schindler gave me life the second time. If not him, I don't think so, or I would have had the chance of survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: What day were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 9th of May, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you and your brother do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of ours, who was just visiting us from Atlanta, who was with me in camp, he grabbed from a German who was running away a buggy and two horses. We loaded it up ourself. It took us seven, eight days to arrive in our hometown in Krakow. Because we were still hoping, maybe, maybe, somebody survived from our family. But to our disappointment, nobody was alive anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you decide to remain in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I spent in Krakow-- the city of Krakow-- five years, till 1950, where in the month of June, 1950, I emigrate to Israel. And I spent two and a half years in Israel, in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I take you back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to know what conditions were like for Jews in Krakow at the time that you went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back, we knew right away that the population in the city is not very sympathetic to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I met a number of people who came before we arrived from the camps. There were a number of people who survived certain camps, like [NON-ENGLISH] from Krakow, where people were liberated already, in June 194-- excuse me-- in January 1945. And from then-- and naturally, wherever we're trying to go, or move, or anything, still we find hatred, and not even one sympathetic person who would be showing his sympathy to us. It happened in 1946, not far from Krakow, in a city called Kielce, where 46 Jews got killed in one afternoon only because they were Jews, and they survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who killed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel it necessary to help yourself by not seeming too Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, we used our name-- from Goldberger we started to use our papers. We had our papers-- Steven and Roman Lesniak. And that's how we still, today, we use the same name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you could have stayed there if you had continued using the name Goldberger?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would have a hard time. Resentment and anti-Semitic remarks and what have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't have any other way. And I had friends and family in Israel. So I went to Israel. And a number of friends and a cousin moved from Israel to Montreal, Canada, where they were writing to us and asking us to come. So we came-- through England-- France and England-- we came to Canada in 1st of September-- excuse me-- 4th of September, 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel, arriving in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt wonderful, because I felt right away, the human being. The approach of the people was completely different. And I felt, that's the country what I would love to live. And I adopt immediately, because they adopt me the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: When that did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife a few months later, when I came to Canada. I arrived in, like I said, September 4th in 1953. And I met my wife in February 1954-- four months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me the names of your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My daughter is Elaine, my son is Stanley.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are there grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are, yes. It's Megan-- that's my grandchild. I have another grandchild. His name is Dylan. And Ari.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started to build, in construction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many people ask about your experiences in the war when you came to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. Many ask me. [DRINKS] But I had a very hard time to relate to them. I had a very hard time to express myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I couldn't-- how could you explain and tell somebody what you went through? It's impossible for anyone who didn't go through the same thing, to understand the horror and the dark days and months and years in our life from 1939 to 1945. It was impossible. Do you think that your experiences during the war affected the way you raised your children and lived your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the beginning. But I was trying to cut out completely my life during the time of the terrible days, because I knew it's a different life. And we, as human, we trying to use all kinds of psychologies and what have you. And we're using it on a daily basis. I felt, in order to my kids not to be affected, never to talk about our past. And I was silent for 49 years. The only thing, when I went to see the movie was something broke in me and I open up. When I saw the movie, I was going through the same thing what I was going on during my six years in concentration camp or ghettos, what have you. My son approached me to go with him. And this happen in December of 1993, immediately after that movie was released. And I was thinking, I can go through the movie without any effect. But I made a big mistake-- because my son's sitting next to me, I broke down terribly. Because each and every scene was the story of my life. Each and every scene I was losing a member of my family. And this man, this Steven Spielberg, helped me terribly, brought me back to my previous life. But still, after the movie ended, I was thankful to the man, because he opened up something what was so closed, so silent during 49 years of my life. By then, I open up, and I started to talk. And I felt much, much better, because I could explain myself because of Steven Spielberg's movie. I remember, like today, when the movie ended, nobody made a move. The silver screen was completely empty. The movie was over. Nobody picked himself up to go out. Finally, after minutes, we walked out-- most of the people with swollen eyes from crying. I'm not talking about Jews, I'm talking about Christians. Because I met many of them later days. And when we walked out of the dim light, and I saw the red dim light exit, I survive again. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But naturally, as we know, six million of our people didn't have the chance to survive. And between them, a million and a half children. This was a big blow to the humanity, because a million and a half young brain who could contribute so much to life, to humanity-- we lost them forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived when so many did not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ask myself this question many times. Even the moment when I looked out-- when they opened the gate, and they-- they-- we were liberated, I put the question against me. And I couldn't explain then. And I was-- I would be unable to explain even today. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years since the end of the war, what is your message to your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: To live a normal life, and try to make the best out of it. Because life is very unpredictable. But it's up to each and every one of us to make a better tomorrow. And that's what I'm telling them. Because they are part of this world, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Steve? Would you like to introduce your wife to us?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I would like to introduce my wife, Esther Lesniak, who's with me the last 37 years. Esther, would you like to say something to Steve?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to say that we never actually-- [CLEARS THROAT] excuse me-- that we never discussed the war before-- 'til, actually, 'til, came on. And ever since that time, we talk about it a lot. And we both realized how precious we are to each other, and how lucky we are to have brought up children. And finally we created our own family, with the three grandchildren. And we pray to God that everything will be OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: May I kiss my wife? [KISS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The lipstick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture, what you see to your left, was taken after the war-- nine months after the war. This was in 1946. That time, I was 46 years-- oh, excuse me. 26 years old. The picture to the right--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in the city of Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: To my right, you see a picture what was taken-- I was interviewed by a French magazine-- and it was taken in the beginning of 1995, after when I received the original Schindler's List from the camp of Brunnlitz. And this happened-- the list is dated actually the 18th of April, 1945, just three weeks before the end of the war. The picture, me pointing, is taken in 1995, in the month of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you pointing to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am pointing now to my name. At that time, it was Goldberger, Saloman. Today, it's Lesniak, Steve.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left of the picture, you can see Oskar Schindler, who was actually the hero. Because of him, 1,100 Jews survive. The picture was taken approximately, the way I see it, in the middle '40s, after the liberation. To my right is Emilie, the wife of Oskar Schindler. The picture was taken two years ago, when she was 88 years old. Today, she's 90 years old. She was born in 1906.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the whole page of [INAUDIBLE] story.

speaker: interviewee
text: This represent the whole page what was printed in the article in the beginning of 1995. It was quite a popular magazine, because I heard they sold out, because of the story. They sold out approximately 15,000 more copies. What you see here is the original list from concentration camp Gross-Rosen made for the Brunnlitz camp. It was done the 18th of April, 1945, just three weeks before the end of the war. By the way, on top in the red, what you see, it's my name and my brother's name listed. How it was listed was the number of the prisoner, the name, the date of birth, and his profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: What place was this list for?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was made for Oskar Schindler to camp in Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people are on the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: 801 men and 300 women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your name on the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name on the list Goldberger, Saloman. And I added S. Lesniak of today. The picture was taken five and a half years ago, in 1990, in my home in Montreal, Canada. Standing to the left is my daughter-in-law, Diane. Next to her, my daughter, Elaine. Next to her, my niece Suzanne. Next, my cousin, Charlotte, my nephew, Irwin. Sitting to the left, my son Stanley, my grandchild, Megan, my nephew's two children-- one is Jason, the other one is Michael. My brother, Roman, who is a survivor of the camp of Oskar Schindler's list, and his late-- late wife, who passed away a year ago, Bernice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1994, in Boca Raton, Florida. To the left is my daughter Elaine. She lives now in Toronto, Canada. To my right is my son, Stanley, who lives now in Boca Raton, Florida. This picture represent myself-- my grandchild to the left-- what her name is Megan. And to the right-- to the right, my nephew's son, Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken five years ago, when they were celebrating my 70 year birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Montreal, Canada, in my private house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandchild. His name is Ari. This was taken two years ago, when he was two and a half years old. It was taken in Toronto, Canada. This picture was taken in Boca Raton, Florida. In the center is my daughter-in-law, Diane. To the left is her child-- my grandchild-- Megan. And to the right is my grandchild. His name is Dylan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, '94.
